title: Isolating Minecraft With Magic and Bubblewrap
summary: Yeah, so, there has been a lot of buzz about Minecraft mods carrying viruses. Let's go ahead and isolate Minecraft from our system. I wrote a script that ought to do everything for you. Download it from Git . To use it, set it as the "wrapper command" in PrismLauncher . Yep. That's it. It wasn't too difficult to make this program. All I did was set an strac…
content:
Yeah, so, there has been a lot of buzz about Minecraft mods carrying viruses. Let's go ahead and isolate Minecraft from our system.

I wrote a script that ought to do everything for you. Download it from Git.

To use it, set it as the "wrapper command" in PrismLauncher.

Yep. That's it.

It wasn't too difficult to make this program. All I did was set an strace off on Minecraft to see what files it looked at, then tweaked the bubblewrap script accordingly.

I'm using NixOS, so that is what is needed to load the files for me. For other Linux distros, it might be a little different, so I just copied in some stuff from the ArchWiki to make it hopefully more cross-platform.

Going through it, we need to load /usr (or /nix/store) because that's where all of the libraries and Java stuff are. I could fine-tune this, but there should not be anything sensitive in there anyway. /dev/dri for graphics, /sys for more graphics, the /run/user stuff for PulseAudio and, you guessed it, graphics. We need to load in the launcher directory because that's where Minecraft and other assets are kept. It just took some trial-and-error (and a lot of strace) to find out why Minecraft kept crashing on launch.

As for X11 security, I can't guarantee that. I tried to nest X11 sessions, but it did not work with 3D acceleration. I guess the solution is to use Wayland or something instead. Perhaps I can later integrate code from x11docker and get GPU acceleration working (but it's an 11k shell script which might take some effort to wrangle).

Update: X11 has now been isolated on Wayland platforms!
